coredata.dc:title: Novel graphene quantum dots modified NH2-MIL-125 photocatalytic composites for effective antibacterial property and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Guoxiang
Wang, Longyang
Zhang, Chencheng
Li, Ping
Du, Hao
Mao, Yanjie
Qiu, Mengyi
Li, Qiang
Hao, Derek
Wang, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection threatens human health, so the design of efficient and stable photocatalytic antibacterial materials has attracted widespread attention. In this regard, a novel photocatalyst GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (GQDs: graphene quantum dots) was prepared by the solvothermal method for photocatalytic disinfection. Since the addition of GQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light and also accelerated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, the photocatalytic performance of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was significantly improved. 5 wt% GQDs loading sample (2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125) exhibited the best antibacterial properties. The killing efficiency by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 against E. coli and S. aureus (107 CFU·mL−1) increased from 40 % and 75 % by NH2-MIL-125 to 92 % and 93 % within 60 min, respectively. Moreover, through the single-wavelength antibacterial experiments, it was found that the introduction of GQDs led to a wide-range-responsive antibacterial effect. Through photocatalytic trapping experiments and ESR analysis, the dominant roles of superoxide radicals (•O2 –) were confirmed in the antibacterial process. Finally, by studying the state of bacteria via TEM, confocal fluorescent images, and gel electrophoresis, it was found that the active substances not only destroyed the cell membranes but also further destroyed DNA to achieve the purpose of completely killing bacteria. This work provides a new method for the rapid disinfection of pathogenic microorganisms using GQDs-related photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MOFs
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photo-disinfection towards E. coli and S. aureus

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21876154 and 22276168 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (JRK22001). The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab ( www.shayanjia.com ) for the TEM tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of (a) NH2-MIL-125 and (b) 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125; (c) TEM image, (d) HRTEM image of GQDs; (e) HRTEM image of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125; (f-k) EDS mapping of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, and (d) pore size distributions of different materials.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra; (b) Bandgap energy; (c) Current-time plots; (d) Nyquist plots of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and related samples.

(a) Antibacterial ratio for E. coil, (b) corresponding cell density, and (c) spread plate results after different treatment times; (d) Antibacterial ratio for S. aureus, (e) corresponding cell density, (f) spread plate results after different treatment times by different samples.

(a) Antibacterial ratio for E. coli, and (b) corresponding cell density by different samples; (c) Antibacterial ratio for E. coli and (d) corresponding cell density by optimized 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 under irradiation with different wavelengths.
(a and b) TEM of E. coli before irradiation; (c and d) TEM of E. coli after 1 h irradiation by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125.

Confocal fluorescent images recorded using dual color channels for live/dead stain at the different times (a) 0 min, (b) 15 min, (c) 30 min, and (d) 60 min of photoinactivation of E. coli with 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 treated.

The effects of radial scavengers on (a) E. coli ratio and (b) corresponding cell density by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125; ESR signals of (c) DMPO-•O2 –, (d) DMPO-•OH, and (e) TEMP-1O2 during photo-disinfection of E. coli by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125; (f) M−S plots of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125;

head.ce:title: Novel graphene quantum dots modified NH2-MIL-125 photocatalytic composites for effective antibacterial property and mechanism insight

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Food Microbial Technology of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater (CTWW), School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Technology Sydney (UTS), Ultimo, NSW 2007, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater (CTWW)
School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater (CTWW), School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Technology Sydney (UTS), Ultimo, NSW 2007, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection threatens human health, so the design of efficient and stable photocatalytic antibacterial materials has attracted widespread attention. In this regard, a novel photocatalyst GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (GQDs: graphene quantum dots) was prepared by the solvothermal method for photocatalytic disinfection. Since the addition of GQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light and also accelerated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, the photocatalytic performance of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was significantly improved. 5 wt% GQDs loading sample (2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125) exhibited the best antibacterial properties. The killing efficiency by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 against E. coli and S. aureus (107 CFU·mL−1) increased from 40 % and 75 % by NH2-MIL-125 to 92 % and 93 % within 60 min, respectively. Moreover, through the single-wavelength antibacterial experiments, it was found that the introduction of GQDs led to a wide-range-responsive antibacterial effect. Through photocatalytic trapping experiments and ESR analysis, the dominant roles of superoxide radicals (•O2 –) were confirmed in the antibacterial process. Finally, by studying the state of bacteria via TEM, confocal fluorescent images, and gel electrophoresis, it was found that the active substances not only destroyed the cell membranes but also further destroyed DNA to achieve the purpose of completely killing bacteria. This work provides a new method for the rapid disinfection of pathogenic microorganisms using GQDs-related photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the serious threat of bacterial contamination to the environment and human health, the development of antibacterial technology has attracted widespread attention. Bacterias are usually divided into two main groups, gram-positive bacteria, and gram-negative bacteria, which are divided according to the structure of the cell wall [1]. Among them, Escherichia coli (E. coli) is gram-negative and widely present in the environment. E. coli is often associated with health risks in water, as the presence of E. coli in water samples is highly to present other harmful viruses and bacteria from feces [2]. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is gram-positive and a deadly pathogen that can cause diseases including skin and soft tissue infections, food poisoning, and even sepsis [3]. Although antibiotic disinfection is currently the most effective method, the misuse of antibiotics can make pathogenic bacteria resistant [4–6]. Therefore, it has become a top priority to develop green and efficient antibacterial technologies. Photocatalytic antibacterial agents have the advantages of high efficiency, being environmentally benign, and being energy efficient [7]. This is because photocatalysts can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) under sunlight illumination [8,9]. To this end, scientists are researching new photocatalysts with high efficiency, wide spectra response, and low toxicity to achieve rapid photocatalytic disinfection.

For more than a decade, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs)-based materials have become a hot spot for photocatalytic antimicrobial applications due to their excellent properties. They have the following advantages compared to conventional antimicrobial materials: (1) Some sterilized metal ions (Ag+, Zn2+, et al.) and organic antimicrobials/natural bio microbial's (imidazole and porphyrin) construct MOFs [10,11]. (2) The highest occupied molecular orbital-lowest unoccupied molecular (HOMO-LUMO) gap is easily replaced by a doped metal or organic components, which is conducive to the formation of various ROS thus achieving high photocatalytic antibacterial efficiency [12,13]. (3) The chelating of MOFs reduces the polarity of their metal ions, thereby enhancing lipophilicity and facilitating MOFs to penetrate the bacterial cell membrane to kill bacteria [14]. (4) MOFs have the characteristics of low toxicity, good dispersion, and biodegradability, which are expected to extend their antimicrobial applications to in vivo research. It has been reported that titanium-based MOFs (such as NH2-MIL-125) as a substrate showed outstanding photocatalytic antibacterial activity. Such as NH2-MIL-125-GO [15], NA/NH2-MIL-125 [16], ZIF-67@ZIF-8@MIL-125-NH2 [17]. However, bare NH2-MIL-125 limits its practical application due to its narrow optical response and low photogenerated transfer efficiency. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a simple and efficient NH2-MIL-125-based photocatalytic antibacterial material.

To address the above issues, quantum dots (QDs) with particle sizes in the nanometer range (typically 2 ∼ 10 nm) have unique structural, electronic, and optical properties that are beneficial for the application in photo-induced electron transfer, up-conversion, and electron reservoir [18,19]. The existence of different types of QDs poses further challenges to the development of various applications, as each type has its unique physicochemical properties. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), obtained from glucose, chitosan, etc., have attracted widespread attention due to their non-toxic, water-soluble, and low-cost properties [20]. Besides, GQDs possess more surface sites and more accessible edges than graphene flakes [21]. Some studies have been conducted on the bactericidal properties of GQDs, showing that GQDs are effective fungicides under visible light [22]. For example, Paszkiewicz-Gawron et al. modified stannates, titanates, and tantalates with GQDs, which finally enhanced the photocatalytic antibacterial activity. It was the deposition of GQDs that enhanced the light capture and thus improved the photoactivity [23]. In addition, GQDs were also applied to modify MOFs for improved photocatalytic properties [24–26]. For example, Liu et al. reported that sensitizing MIL-101(Fe) by GQDs could significantly promote the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CO. Because the presence of GQDs could promote separation and migration of photogenerated charges of MIL-101(Fe) [24]. Titanium-based MOFs have non-toxic properties and are more likely to form more stable coordination bonds with organic ligands than most transition metal-based MOFs, and they also have Ti-O structures similar to those found in TiO2 [27]. Therefore, modifying NH2-MIL-125 by GQDs is expected not only to improve the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges but also to provide new active sites. However, so far, there has been limited research on the regulation and mechanism of the photocatalytic antibacterial process by GQDs-modified MOFs. Furthermore, when GQDs are distributed on the surface of NH2-MIL-125, it can effectively overcome the shortcomings of the GQDs and MOFs. Because the combination of NH2-MIL-125 and GQDs not only improves the visible light absorption capacity of NH2-MIL-125 but also accelerates the transport of photogenerated electrons and inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers.

Herein, a novel visible-light-active catalyst (GQDs/NH2-MIL-125) was prepared by the solvothermal method. E. coli (gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria) are among the most studied organisms because they are of great clinical and scientific interest. The photocatalytic performance was evaluated via antibacterial effects against E. coli and S. aureus. The antibacterial activity of the photocatalyst was adjusted by changing the loading percentage of GQDs. The antibacterial properties were also studied by changing the wavelength of incident light. The structure, optical properties, and photocatalytic antibacterial activity of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were studied in depth. The state of bacteria during the photocatalytic process was studied via HRTEM, confocal fluorescent images, and gel electrophoresis, to monitor the possible structural changes and generation of active species.

Experimental chemicals are commercially available and have an analytical purity (AR) that does not require further purification. S. aureus was purchased from Qingdao Haibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. E. coil was purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbiology Co., Ltd. Soluble starch was purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd; Agar powder was purchased from The Institute of Jiancheng Biological Engineering in Nanjing. Beef extract and Peptone were purchased from Hangzhou Microbial Reagent Co., Ltd; 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid (ATA), p-Phthalic acid (PTA), Titanium butoxide, 2-Methyl-1,4-benzene dicarboxylic acid, and N, N-Dimethylformamide was purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). Sodium sulfite was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. p-Benzoquinone, and Isopropyl Alcohol was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The preparation process has been optimized according to previous reports [28,29]. 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid (ATA) and DMF were mixed in a single-mouth flask. After heating at 105 °C and stirring for 1 h with the bottle open to dissolve the acid and remove the residual moisture, 14 mL of anhydrous methanol was added. Then, 4.2 mL of tetra butyl titanate was added to the solution keeping stirred at 100 °C for 72 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature and filtered, where the white solid was separated by centrifugation. After washing with DMF at 155 °C for 24 h, then washing with methanol at 100 °C to remove DMF, the solid was finally dried for 12 h at 70 °C. In this way, NH2-MIL-125 was obtained.
The synthesis procedures of MIL-125 were similar to the method described above. The only difference was that 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid (ATA) was replaced with p-Phthalic acid (PTA).

The preparation method for GQDs was optimized according to previous reports [30]. First, starch (3 g) was added into deionized water (300 mL), followed by 30 min water bath at 60 °C. Subsequently, the starch solution was moved into the polytetrafluoroethylene kettle body and kept at 190 °C for 2 h. The reacted liquid was centrifuged at 15,000 r/min for 5 min, and the pale-yellow supernatant was the GQDs solution.

X (X = 0, 1.5, 2.5, 5, 7.5) mL GQDs solution and (20-X) mL deionized water were mixed to obtain composites with different ratios, labeled as Y-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Y = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4). The as-prepared NH2-MIL-125 (200 mg of each part) was added to the above solution and stirred for 12 h. The resultant precipitate was separated via centrifugation at speed of 1000 r/min for 5 min and then washed 3 times with deionized water. Finally, the obtained sample was dried at 70 °C for 6 h, denoted as GQDs/NH2-MIL-125. Among them, 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was the best sample.

Crystal structures were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Cu Kα radiation with λ of 0.15406 nm and the scanning ranged from 5 to 45°, Bruker D8 advance). The pore structure and specific surface area (BET) of the material were characterized by a specific surface area analyzer (Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Regulus8100) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20) were used to observe morphological changes after the introduction of GQDs in NH2-MIL-125. The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectrum of the sample was characterized using a spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere using the TU-1901 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer. The chemical bonds of the sample were characterized using a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet iS 10). Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. The thermal stability and GQDs content of the samples were analyzed by a Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Setaram Labsys Evo). The photoelectrochemical properties were measured with three electrodes on CHI 760E electrochemical station. ESR was performed with a Bruker ESR ELEXSYS 500 spectrometer. To observe the morphological changes of E. coli at different times under the action of a photocatalyst, a transmission electron microscope (TEM, HITACHI-7650) was used for observation. The photocatalytic destruction of E. coli cell membranes was analyzed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, LEICA SP8, Germany).

The gram-negative E. coli and gram-positive S. aureus were selected as the research objects, and the photocatalytic sterilization performance of the samples was evaluated. In a typical procedure, E. coli (or S. aureus) was cultured in 100 mL liquid Luria-Bertani broth for 12 h at 37 °C. Dilute the bacterial stock solution with a liquid medium until the UV spectrophotometer is set at 600 nm, the absorbance of the bacterial solution is about 0.5, and take 5 mL of OD600 = 0.5. This bacterial solution is then centrifuged in a high-speed centrifuge for 5 min at 8000 rpm. The supernatant is isolated and sterilized saline is the bacterial suspension (50 mL). The above bacterial suspension was placed in a 50 mL beaker with a 20 mg target catalyst and stirred on a magnetic stirrer until the bacteria and catalyst were mixed evenly, then stirred constantly under 100 W LED light (λ = 370–780 nm) for 1 h. Finally, diluted 108-fold 100 μL of bacterial liquid medium and spread evenly on a nutritious agar plate for 24 h at 37 °C.

ROS quenching experiment was referred to in the previous report [16]. There is a series of scavengers, among which, sodium chromate (Cr(VI), 0.5 mM) for e-, p-benzoquinone (0.1 mM) for •O2 –, isopropanol (10 mM) for •OH, sodium oxalate (10 mM) for h+, l-histidine (10 mM) for 1O2 and nitrogen (10 mL/min) exclusion oxygen. The scavengers and 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 nanocomposite were added together into the E. coli solution (5 mL OD600 = 0.5 bacterial culture solution was centrifuged and diluted into 50 mL 0.9 % NaCl) before irradiation. The remaining conditions were the same as the bacteriostatic test.

ROS generated by GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was detected by the ESR technique. Before recording, the suspension containing 2-QGDs/NH2-MIL-125 with spin probes (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidine (TEMP) was placed in water or methanol solution and irradiated with a 100 W LED light for a certain time interval.

The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 . The detailed preparation process is described in the experiment section above. The morphologies of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and related catalysts were shown in Fig. 2
(a, b), respectively. It can be observed that NH2-MIL-125 was disc-shaped, with a length and thickness of around 500 and 100 nm, respectively. In addition, the boundaries of this material presented a non-smooth cotton-like shape. However, due to the limitations in SEM resolution ( Fig. 2 (b)), GQDs on NH2-MIL-125 were not visible from the image. The morphology of GQDs was studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image shows that the particle size of GQDs is less than 5 nm, the distribution is narrow, and the main particle size range is 1.0–2.5 nm. And the highest proportion of size is arranged around 1.5–2.0 nm (Fig. 2 (c) inset). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of GQDs (Fig. 2 (d)) demonstrates clear lattice structures, and the lattice spaces around 0.24 nm (Fig. 2 (d) inset), corresponding to (1120) lattice stripes of graphene [31]. The (1120) lattice stripes of graphene are also present in 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. 2 (e)). Furthermore, as for GQDs/NH2-MIL-125, the loading of GQDs was also obvious via TEM investigation (
Fig. S1
). In addition, EDS mapping studies were carried out on 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Fig. 2 (f-k)). The C, N, O, and Ti elements were observed to be evenly distributed. The above results effectively proved that GQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of NH2-MIL-125.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 composites with different dosages of GQDs were shown in Fig. 3
(a). As for NH2-MIL-125, the XRD patterns were consistent with previously reported results, indicating successful synthesis [29]. The XRD patterns of GQDs showed that there was a broad peak near 24 Å, corresponding to the (002) peak. It matched well with the JCPDS card (No. 75–0444) [32], indicating that the starch generated a graphene structure through a hydrothermal reaction. For the GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 composite, the XRD peaks showed only the diffraction peaks of NH2-MIL-125, which may be due to the overlap of NH2-MIL-125 with the characteristic peaks of GQDs. With increasing GQDs content, the characteristic peaks of NH2-MIL-125 were well maintained, but with slight decrease in intensity.

To further explore the chemical structural changes of the catalyst after GQDs loading, the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were studied and tested. As shown in Fig. 3 (b), CO telescopic vibration peaks (1338 cm−1) and CO telescopic vibrations (1535 cm−1) were present in 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 [33]. The CC telescopic vibration of NH2-MIL-125 at 1606 cm−1 was enhanced in 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125, which was attributed to glucose dehydration in GQDs [34]. At the same time, the CN telescopic vibration single peak at 1250 cm−1 was also enhanced [35]. The peak at 400–800 cm−1 corresponded to the tensile vibration of the O-Ti-O bond [33]. The peak at 1084 cm−1 was vibration from the CO key in GQDs [32]. Therefore, the FT-IR results demonstrated that GQDs have been successfully anchored to NH2-MIL-125. Furthermore, Fig. S2 shows that there is a negligible difference in the FT-IR spectra of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 before and after use, indicating good structural stability of the as-prepared composite.

It is well known the high specific surface area of the catalyst directly affects the photoactivity and adsorption capacity. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 showed a type I character [29], indicating microporous nature ( Fig. 3 (c)). According to the results of BET analysis, the specific surface area (S BET) of NH2-MIL-125 and 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was ca. 1503.0 and 1193.5 m2·g−1, respectively. The pore volumes (Vp) of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and NH2-MIL-125 were ca. 0.72 and 0.86 cm3·g−1 (Fig. 3 (d)), respectively. However, according to the pore size distribution calculated by the non-local density functional theory method (NLDFT) [36], the pore size distribution of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and NH2-MIL-125 remained almost unchanged. The pore sizes were mainly in the range of 0.5–1.2 nm (Fig. 3 (d)). The above results indicate that the GQDs combined with NH2-MIL-125 can reduce the S BET and Vp of NH2-MIL-125. Furthermore, TG analysis of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and NH2-MIL-125 showed that they had good thermal stability. As found in Fig. S3 , the weightless tendency of the samples was relatively flat before 335 °C, which might be caused by the evaporation of the residual solvent [37]. Between 335 °C and 500 °C, the samples experienced significant weightlessness, which was attributed to the decomposition of metal–organic bonds and the collapse of the NH2-MIL-125 structure. When the heating temperature exceeded 465 °C, 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and NH2-MIL-125 were converted to TiO2, and the mass difference was about 5 wt%, which indicated that the content of GQDs was about 5 wt%. In summary, GQDs was successfully loaded onto NH2-MIL-125 with good stability.

The UV–vis DRS spectra of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 and related samples are shown in Fig. 4
(a). It was clear that the NH2-MIL-125 had a dominant peak near 400 nm and an absorption edge near ca. 530 nm. 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 exhibited a good response in the visible and near-infrared regions, mainly due to the optical absorption properties of GQDs. According to the (αhv)2-hv and αhv = A(hv-Eg)n/2
[38,39], the band gap of different composites is calculated, as shown in the illustration of Fig. 4 (b). The band gaps of NH2-MIL-125 and 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were ca. 2.69 and 2.47 eV, respectively. The results show that depositing GQDs can reduce the band gap and extend the light absorption range. Photocurrent and EIS analysis can be used to characterize the separation process of charge carriers in semiconductor materials. These results further confirm the effect of interfacial charge transfer. GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 exhibited a high photocurrent response since GQDs can be used as electron reservoirs, which effectively reduces the recombination of e- and h+ pairs. With the increase of GQDs loading percentage, the photocurrent density shows a trend of increasing first and then decreasing, of which 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 had the highest optical value. To further explore the optical response of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 composite photocatalysts, PL spectroscopy was performed at an excitation wavelength of 385 nm. As shown in Fig. S4 , each sample forms a mainly characteristic peak at 469 nm. With the increase of GQDs loading, the excitation intensity was first decreased and then increased, with the lowest peak at 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125. This indicated that the photoexcited electron recombination rate of NH2-MIL-125 was the highest, while the recombination rate of photoexcited electrons of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was significantly reduced. According to the PL results, the GQDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the arc radius in the EIS Nyquist diagram reflects the separation effect of photogenerated e- - h+ pairs during interfacial charge transfer and hybridization [40,41]. As can be seen from the Nyquist diagram ( Fig. 4 (d)), the radius of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 nanocomposites was the smallest. This indicates that 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 has the lowest resistance, which is conducive to promoting the separation and transfer of interfacial charges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-disinfection towards E. coli and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial effect of as-prepared samples were determined by the plate coating method. The optimal photocatalyst was preliminarily obtained by the difference in the photocatalytic antibacterial performance of Y-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Y = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) of different GQDs loading percentages. The bacteriostatic effects were used to conduct antibacterial experiments on two bacteria using a 0.4 g·L-1 photocatalyst. Before the photocatalytic antibacterial experiment, to exclude the influence of light, blank control experiments were performed with LED light only (without adding photocatalyst). From the results, it can be seen that the antibacterial rates of E. coli and S. aureus were less than 3 % when only 100 W LED light was illuminated for 60 min ( Fig. S5 ), indicating that the effect of light alone on bacteria was very small. Then, experimental results with photocatalysts for E. coil and S. aureus are shown in Fig. 5
(a-c) and Fig. 5 (d-f), respectively. There was no significant change in the number of bacteria after 30 min under dark conditions, and the decrease was within 3 %, which indicates that the catalyst itself does not have a significant antibacterial effect.

Under the illumination of 60 min, the 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 composite displayed excellent antibacterial effects for both E. coil and S. aureus, which can kill more than 90 % of bacterias with a concentration of 107 CFU·mL−1. For comparison, single NH2-MIL-125 was also applied to E. coli and S. aureus. However, low antibacterial activity was obtained. The value for E. coli and S. aureus inactivation by NH2-MIL-125 were 35 % and 60 %, respectively. Therefore, the special role of GQDs loading can be confirmed. Besides, as for single NH2-MIL-125, the difference for E. coli and S. aureus inactivation may be induced by adsorption and structure of bacteria. For example, the cell wall of gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) is thicker, about 20–80 nm. The content of peptidoglycan is rich, there are 15 ∼ 50 layers, accounting for 50 ∼ 80 % of the cell dry weight. In addition, there are a large number of special components of teichoic acid. The negatively charged teichoic acid can interact with the metal ion (Ti4+) in NH2-MIL-125, which led to stronger adsorption than E. coli and subsequent higher photocatalytic activity.

To further explore the photocatalytic antibacterial activities of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 against E. coli., the photocatalytic antibacterial properties of GQDs, MIL-125, NH2-MIL-125, and 2-GQDs/MIL-125 were tested respectively ( Fig. 6
(a, b)). Among them, GQDs, MIL-125, and NH2-MIL-125 showed poor antibacterial performance against E. coli (the bacterial efficiency was lower than 35 % within 60 min). The antibacterial activity of 2-GQDs/MIL-125 and 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were 60 % and 92 %, respectively. This indicated that GQDs combined with MIL-125 could improve the photocatalytic antibacterial activity against E. coli. Moreover, 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 could further improve the antibacterial properties due to the introduction of the –NH2 group.

To study the effects of light wavelengths on the antibacterial properties of E. coli by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125, antibacterial experiments were conducted under LED light with different wavelengths ( Fig. 6 (c, d)). It can be observed that 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 had negligible antibacterial effects under dark conditions. When 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was irradiated with white light, the antibacterial performance was the best, mainly because white light had a wider wavelength range (380–840 nm). Interestingly, the antibacterial properties decreased with the increase in wavelength. This is mainly because the absorption of light energy by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 under different light wavelengths is not the same, which is consistent with the results of UV–vis DRS analysis. In addition, it also had an antibacterial effect under near-infrared light, indicating that the introduction of GQDs broadens the spectral response range of NH2-MIL-125.

Moreover, Fig. S6 shows the E. coli test for five cycles by 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 under LED illumination for 60 mins. It can be seen that the 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 still maintain high photocatalytic antibacterial activity after 5 cycles. The results showed that the sample had good photochemical stability.

It is generally believed that the cause of bacterial death during photocatalysis may be the destruction of the cell wall and cell membrane by ROS. These ROS also further damage the genetic material within the bacteria [42,43]. Therefore, to elucidate the antibacterial mechanism of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 against E. coli, the changes in bacteria were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and fluorescent live/dead staining. Cell membranes were intact and smooth before light illumination ( Fig. 7
(a, b)). After 60 min of light exposure, the bacterial cell membranes were gradually twisted, broken, and destroyed. The damaged cell membrane allowed more ROS to enter the bacteria, breaking down the bacteria’s genetic material. Furthermore, to reveal the integrity of cells and the photocatalytic activity of inactivated E. coli, corresponding fluorescent-based live/dead staining of dual acridine orange/propidium iodide (AO/PI) dyes was performed [44]. AO can rapidly penetrate intact living cell membranes, binding to DNA and revealing “green” fluorescence. In contrast, PI can only show “red” fluorescence by destroying cell membranes that bind to DNA. Almost all green fluorescence was visible in the dark treatment group, indicating that all live cells have a complete cell membrane under dark treatment ( Fig. 8
(a)). After irradiation for 15 min, part of the red fluorescence replaced the green fluorescence, indicating that part of the cell membrane was destroyed by ROS. Obviously, with the prolonging of the irradiation time, the red fluorescence was increasing, and the red fluorescence almost completely replaced the green fluorescence after 1 h reaction. These results indicate that the cell membrane was destroyed by the ROS, which coincides with the results of the morphological change of the cell envelope observed by HRTEM.

To study the photocatalytic antibacterial process, there may be a variety of different ROS. Thus, different quenching agents, such as isopropanol (quenching •OH), sodium oxalate (quenching h+), Cr(VI) (quenching e-), p-benzoquinone (quenching •O2 –), l-histidine (quenching 1O2), and nitrogen (exclusion oxygen) in the solution can be used to explore inactivation mechanism of E. coli. To exclude the effect of the quenching agent on E. coli, we only added an appropriate concentration of quenching agent (without adding a catalyst) for blank control experiments and found that almost all quenching agents had little effect on E. coli ( Fig. S8 ), indicating that an appropriate concentration of quenching agent would not cause harm to E. coli. When p-benzoquinone was added, the antibacterial properties of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were significantly reduced, indicating that •O2 – is the main active substance. In addition, when isopropanol and l-histidine were added, the antibacterial activity of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 also showed a certain degree of reduction, which indicates that •OH and 1O2 are also involved in the antibacterial process ( Fig. 9
(a, b)).

Furthermore, the generation of ROS was also verified via the ESR technique. As shown in Fig. 9 (c-e), there were no obvious DMPO-•O2 –, DMPO-•OH, and TEMP-1O2 signals in the dark. However, under light illumination, 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 showed typical signals of •O2 –, •OH, and 1O2. This result is consistent with the photocatalytic trapping experiments.

To further explore the photocatalytic reaction mechanism, we performed Mott-Schottky plots. The energy level position of the prepared catalysts was obtained by testing the flat band potential. The 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 sample exhibited a positive slope, suggesting that it is an n-type semiconductor ( Fig. 9 (f)). The Mott-Schottky measurements showed that the flat band position (Efb) of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was −0.84 V (vs Ag/AgCl). Herein, the NHE potential is converted by EAg/AgCl of the electrode potential by the following equation (Eq.) [29,47]:
(1)
E
NHE
=
E
Ag / A g C l
+ Δ V + 0.0591
7 - p H
Where △V = 0.197 V is the standard potential of Ag/AgCl relative to NHE. Since the bottom of the conduction band (CB) in n-type semiconductors is often 0.1 V negative than Efb [48], the CB potential of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 is calculated to be −0.74 V (vs NHE). Combined with the results of UV–vis DRS, the band gap width of 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 is 2.47 V, so its valence band (VB) position was estimated to be 1.73 V ( Fig. 10
). However, the flat band position of NH2-MIL-125 is −0.64 V (vs NHE) (
Fig. S9
). According to calculations, the CB of NH2-MIL-125 was −0.54 V (vs NHE). Because the band gap of NH2-MIL-125 is 2.69 V, its VB was calculated to be ca. 2.15 V (vs NHE). Therefore, firstly, GQDs enhance visible light absorption. Secondly, it is conducive to the transfer of photoexcitation electrons from the CB of NH2-MIL-125 to GQDs, a process that greatly inhibits the recombination of light-producing carriers.

Therefore, we proposed a possible mechanism for photocatalytic antibacterial action. The band structure and antibacterial mechanism of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were summarized in Fig. 10. Under light irradiation, the e- of VB in GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 were easily transitioned to CB, while h+ remained in VB. Subsequently, the e- in CB could react with O2 forming •O2 – due to the more negative potential of CB relative to the redox potential of O2/•O2 – (-0.33 V vs NHE) [49]. Then, •O2 – could react with h+ forming 1O2, since the VB potential of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 (+1.73 V vs NHE) could not generate •OH by oxidizing H2O (E (•OH/H2O) = +2.34 V) and OH– (E (•OH/OH–) = +1.99 V) [50]. However, ESR spectroscopy showed that GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 could produce •OH. Because the reduction potential of O2/H2O2 in GQD/NH2-MIL-125 was + 0.69 V vs NHE [51], photogenerated e- in CB could also reduce the adsorbed O2 form H2O2·H2O2 can be further decomposed into •OH [52]. Based on the above analysis, it is speculated that the generation of these ROSs may involve Eqs. (2)–(7):
(2) GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 + hv → e- + h+
(3) O2 + e- → •O2 –
(4) •O2 – + 2H+ + e- → H2O2
(5) H2O2 + e- → •OH + OH–
(6) •O2 – + h+ → 1O2
(7) Live bacteria+ •O2 – + •OH + 1O2 → Dead bacteria

Thus, the reasons for the excellent antibacterial performance of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 are: (1) the presence of a large number of active sites (GQDs) on the surface of NH2-MIL-125 and the strong absorption of O2 are conducive to the production of ROS; (2) the porous structure of NH2-MIL-125 improves the dispersion and stability of GQDs; (3) the combination of GQDs and NH2-MIL-125 enhances the light absorption range; (4) the introduction of GQDs promote the transfer of photogenerated e-. Further studies confirmed that the main antibacterial mechanism of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 is achieved by generating ROS under light irradiation. First, the resulting ROS, such as •O2 –, oxidizes and destroys the cell walls and membranes of bacteria, leading to degradation and leakage of biomolecules such as DNA. Finally, the synergistic effect of NH2-MIL-125 and GQDs leads to the rapid inactivation of bacteria.

In this work, a novel GQD/NH2-MIL-125 photocatalyst was synthesized by the solvothermal method. Since the addition of GQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light and also accelerated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, the photocatalytic performance of GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 was significantly improved. Among which, the 2-GQDs/NH2-MIL-125 exhibited the best antibacterial properties and can work under spectral range. Through capture experiments and ESR analysis, it was also confirmed that •O2 – was the main active species in the antibacterial process. Finally, it was found that the ROS not only destroyed the cell membranes but also further destroyed DNA to achieve the purpose of completely killing bacteria. The present work may provide useful information for the design of new materials that efficiently remove microbial contaminants under light irradiation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shayanjia.com) for the TEM tests.
